title: Creating, editing, and displaying members in ISPF

segments.text: So, now that we have a PDS dataset, you probably want to do something with it.
In this lesson, we'll go through the process of creating, editing, and displaying members
in a PDS.
We're going to be using the Edit Entry panel, and you get there from option 2 off of the
main ISPF panel.
To create a new dataset member in an existing PDS, enter the name of the dataset on the
Edit Entry panel.
And again, you can see here you can use the handy three fields, or you can enter the full
dataset name into the field below it.
Notice that there's a fourth field in the bottom for member.
That's where you put the name of the member that you're creating.
Or if you're using the field below, you're going to spell out the name of the member
at the end of the PDS in parentheses.
For example, i-n-s-t-j-r-g-dot-e-s-ten-dot-p-d-s-parentheses-member-one-parentheses, where the first three parts
make up the PDS name, and member one is the name of the member.
So fill those out either way, and then hit enter.
This will open up the ISPF editor.
To start typing into this member, move your cursor into the empty area towards the right,
and then start typing.
You can type on multiple lines, just don't type over on the left column yet.
When you're done, hit enter, and you'll see numbers appear over on the left there.
These are the record numbers, and when you want to save your masterpiece, type save up
top and hit enter.
The top line of your screen shows the name of the dataset and the member that you're
editing.
This is a good way to verify that you have the correct dataset and member selected.
To display the members in a dataset, go back to that edit entry panel.
Put in the name of the dataset without specifying any members, and hit enter.
In this example, there are six members in the dataset i-n-s-t-j-r-g-dot-e-s-ten-dot-p-d-s.
We can edit any of those by placing the letter e next to them and then hitting enter.
This brings up the editor again.
Another way to display the members in a dataset is from the dataset list utility.
You get there from the first ISPF screen, option three for utilities, and then option
four for DS list.
From the DS list utility screen, enter the high level qualifier in the DS name level
field, and don't enter anything in the option field, just hit enter.
It'll come back and show you all of the datasets that start with that high level qualifier.
You can then tab to the left of the dataset you want to view and enter a forward slash.
A list of options will pop up.
Option four of that list is member list, and that's just what we want, so type four and
hit enter.
What if you're in a dataset member and you want to add some new lines to it, some new
records?
That's easy, and we get to use that left column for this one.
Move your cursor over to the line you want to add lines underneath and type an i.
A new line appears, and here's a helpful tip.
If you want to add five lines, you type i5 and then hit enter, and you can do the same
kind of thing for eight lines, nine lines, or 20 lines, but let's not get too crazy here.
You can now add data to these lines, just like you did when you were first creating
that member.
Hit enter when you're done, and those lines with data get turned into records, and any
lines with no data, they go away.
We can delete lines too.
Here we have a member called member two.
To delete a line, place a single d followed by a space next to that line.
Now, the reason for the space is that it's just safer.
We know that we're only deleting one line that way.
Hit enter, and then boom, it's gone.
And just like insert, if we want to delete five lines, I can type d5.
You get it, pretty simple stuff.
ISPF also has block commands, so to move a block of text, enter mm on the first line
of text you want to move, and then another mm on the last line of text you want to move.
Then in the spot where you want to move it, place an a for after or a b or before to tell
it whether you want to move that block of text before or after that line.
As you can imagine, there are a lot of line commands we can use in the editor.
Here's a listing of just some of them which you can use during your lab exercises.
